name: UK Ministry of Defence — AI
overview: The UK MoD treats AI as a strategic priority, coordinated centrally through the Defence AI Centre (DAIC). Its public footprint is strongest on strategy, policy and procurement — a published AI strategy (2022), a Playbook (2024) and a safety/assurance framework (JSP 936, 2024) — and on building an experimentation and supplier pipeline (the 'Foundry' data environment, the AI Model Arena, DASA competitions, and large open frameworks like Asgard). Fielded operational systems are mostly undisclosed; what's public is the scaffolding, the money, and the contracts.

established.label: The in-house HQ for AI
established.plain: Think of it as the MoD's central AI headquarters. Instead of every part of the military buying and building AI on its own, the DAIC coordinates it all — sets the direction, runs shared projects, and is the front door connecting Defence with industry and universities. Stood up after the 2021 Integrated Review; running since 2022.

established.label: The official plan
established.plain: The UK's public statement of how it wants to use AI in defence — and how it won't. It set the ambition: be ready for AI, adopt it fast, and do it responsibly. It's the north star everything since hangs off, not a product you can point at.

established.label: The how-to guide
established.plain: The strategy says WHAT; the Playbook says HOW. A practical handbook for teams across Defence on actually adopting AI — getting from an idea to a fielded tool — so people aren't reinventing the wheel every time.

established.label: The safety rulebook
established.plain: The rulebook for doing AI safely and responsibly. Before an AI system can be used, it has to meet these standards — is it reliable, is someone accountable, can we trust it under pressure. These are the guardrails that let Defence move fast without being reckless.

established.label: The data engine room
established.plain: The engine room — a secure environment where Defence's data is brought together so AI models can actually be built and run on it. It's already been used to crunch data for the 2025 Strategic Defence Review. Without this plumbing, the strategy stays words on paper.
established.sourceName: Parliament — Developing AI capacity in UK Defence

inProgress.claim: AI Model Arena — a tool to redefine how Defence evaluates and procures AI, built with UK firm Advai and the National Security Strategic Investment Fund; targeted live by DAIC Connect (March 2026).
inProgress.sourceName: GOV.UK — Launching the AI Model Arena

inProgress.claim: Project Asgard — 26 companies awarded a four-year 'Open Framework' to build AI/ML decision-support for military targeting (the 'Decide' step) for the British Army (tender July 2025).
UK Defence Innovation (UKDI) created by the Strategic Defence Review 2025 with a ring-fenced budget of at least £400M/yr to harness commercial and dual-use technology, including AI.
DASA 'Intelligent Ship — The Next Generation' competition funding AI to tackle crew information-overload (£4M programme, nine first-wave projects).

latest.claim: Asgard AI targeting framework drew public scrutiny over autonomy and ethics as the 26-company award was reported.
Alan Turing Institute published a Defence AI Assurance study (system cards / promising practice) with the MoD — signalling a push on trustworthy AI.

suppliers.name: Faculty (Faculty Science Ltd)
suppliers.role: AI specialist — partner on Dstl's £7M AI experimentation contract

suppliers.name: Kainos
suppliers.role: Prime on Dstl's AI experimentation contract (NI-based; up to 20 data-science roles)

suppliers.name: Actica Consulting
suppliers.role: Defence/AI delivery partner on the Dstl experimentation contract

suppliers.name: Advai
suppliers.role: UK AI assurance firm — building the AI Model Arena with DAIC + NSSIF

suppliers.name: Asgard framework (26 suppliers)
suppliers.role: Four-year open framework for AI/ML military-targeting decision support (British Army)

unknowns: Fielded, operational AI systems are largely classified — the public domain shows strategy, policy, money and procurement, not deployed capability. Most of Asgard's 26 suppliers are unnamed publicly. Outputs of the Foundry and specific operational use cases are not disclosed.
